title: Towards a Pseudo-Labeling Workflow for Celltype-Classification from Explanted Brain Slice Recordings
summary: This paper proposes an unsupervised workflow to pseudo-label extracellular spikes from human brain slice MEA recordings into two putative cell types: pyramidal cells and interneurons. Here, the raw data from the data acquisition system is used and processed. The pipeline for pre-processing includes bandpass filtering, threshold--based spike detection, frame alignment and normalization. In the ML workflow, dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP), clustering (GMM, k-means). To achieve an online system, template matching and OSort under varying curation strictness is also considered. All pipelines are evaluated by different cluster quality with within-cluster Pearson correlation, Silhouette score, and Calinski-Harabasz index. Applying stricter curation improves separation at some cost to inclusivity.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an unsupervised machine learning workflow to classify extracellular spikes from human brain slice MEA recordings into pyramidal cells and interneurons using dimensionality reduction, clustering, and template matching methods, with stricter curation improving separation at the cost of inclusivity.
AI.motivation: To develop an unsupervised, automated method for classifying extracellular spikes into cell types (pyramidal cells vs. interneurons) from MEA recordings, eliminating the need for manual curation and enabling online processing.
AI.method: Raw MEA data undergoes bandpass filtering, threshold-based spike detection, frame alignment, and normalization. Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP) and clustering (GMM, k-means) are applied. Template matching and OSort under varying curation strictness are explored for online deployment. Cluster quality is evaluated using within-cluster Pearson correlation, Silhouette score, and Calinski-Harabasz index.
AI.result: Stricter curation improves cluster separation (higher quality metrics) but reduces inclusivity (fewer spikes classified). Different dimensionality reduction and clustering combinations yield varying trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: An unsupervised ML pipeline can effectively pseudo-label spikes into two putative cell types from MEA data, but there is a trade-off between separation quality and inclusivity that must be managed based on application needs.

title: Esports and Physiological Tremor a StarCraft 2 Tournament Study
summary: Physiological tremor of the upper limb is a sensitive neuromuscular indicator that may be modulated by cognitive load and competitive stress, yet its behaviour in real esports conditions remains uncharacterised. We measured wrist accelerometer-based tremor in 16 healthy adult male StarCraft~2 players across two tournament days, computing log power spectral density ($log(PSD)$) and dominant frequency in four bands (2--4, 8--14, 10--20, and 1--25Hz) and comparing them to published population norms using linear mixed models. Players deviated significantly from the reference in all bands: $log(PSD)$ was elevated at 2--4~Hz and substantially reduced at higher frequencies (Cohen's $d = 1.6$--$2.3$), suggesting long-term neuromuscular adaptation to the fine-motor demands of esports. Tremor indicators declined systematically over the tournament day. Contrary to the fatigue-related increases typical of traditional motor tasks. Neither game outcome nor actions per minute significantly predicted post-game tremor. These findings suggest physiological tremor may reflect a generalised psychophysiological adaptation to competitive esports rather than being a short-term performance predictor.

AI.tldr: This study measured wrist tremor in 16 StarCraft 2 players during tournaments and found they deviated from population norms (elevated low-frequency tremor, reduced high-frequency tremor), with tremor declining over the day contrary to typical fatigue effects, suggesting long-term neuromuscular adaptation rather than short-term performance prediction.
AI.motivation: To characterize physiological tremor of the upper limb in real esports conditions, as it may be modulated by cognitive load and competitive stress but its behaviour in such settings was previously uncharacterised.
AI.method: Measured wrist accelerometer-based tremor in 16 healthy adult male StarCraft 2 players across two tournament days, computing log power spectral density and dominant frequency in four frequency bands, comparing to population norms using linear mixed models.
AI.result: Players deviated significantly from reference norms in all bands: log(PSD) elevated at 2-4 Hz and reduced at higher frequencies (Cohen's d = 1.6-2.3). Tremor indicators declined systematically over the tournament day, contrary to typical fatigue-related increases. Game outcome and actions per minute did not significantly predict post-game tremor.
AI.conclusion: Physiological tremor may reflect a generalised psychophysiological adaptation to competitive esports rather than serving as a short-term performance predictor.

title: Recovering Candidate Circadian Regulators of Arrhythmic Pituitary Hormone Genes Using Reliability-Weighted Magnetic Laplacian with rwMagLap
summary: We study how to recover candidate circadian-clock regulators of pituitary hormone genes that are important for women's health but do not show a clear 24-hour rhythm in bulk tissue, aiming to nominate clock-linked regulatory targets that could inform future chronopharmacologic and chronotherapeutic strategies. We propose \textbf{rwMagLap}, which builds a graph on rhythmic backbone genes. For each edge, we combine 24-hour fit quality with peak-time phase, represented as a complex unit-circle value, yielding a Hermitian adjacency matrix and a magnetic Laplacian. We insert arrhythmic hormone genes, treated as anchors, by a reliability-weighted nearest-neighbor projection. The projected anchor-neighbor weights are pooled into a soft teleport distribution, and complex personalized PageRank then ranks rhythmic backbone genes by the magnitude of their PageRank scores. In pituitary data, we find that all 11 women's-health anchors are arrhythmic. Even so, we find that the top-50 list is $7.95\times$ enriched for the 13-gene KEGG circadian set (7 of the 8 set genes in the 454-gene backbone; corrected Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) $p_{\mathrm{BH}}=4\times10^{-6}$) and $4.54\times$ enriched for the 111-gene Reactome set (8 of 16 genes; $p_{\mathrm{BH}}=1.6\times10^{-4}$), while a phase-blind real-valued baseline recovers none. We recover candidates through reliability weighting and phase-aware seeding rather than through magnetic propagation. The magnetic phase adds a different capability: it represents temporal order. On pituitary backbone, the magnetic embedding recovers measured peak-time order of connected pituitary genes with accuracy $0.971$, while $q{=}0$, i.e., no magnetic charge, is at chance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes rwMagLap, a method using magnetic Laplacians and complex PageRank on rhythmic gene graphs to recover circadian-clock regulators of arrhythmic hormone genes, demonstrating strong enrichment for circadian gene sets and accurate phase recovery in pituitary data.
AI.motivation: To recover candidate circadian-clock regulators of pituitary hormone genes that are arrhythmic (lack clear 24-hour rhythm) in bulk tissue, aiming to identify clock-linked targets for future chronopharmacologic and chronotherapeutic strategies for women's health.
AI.method: Builds a graph on rhythmic backbone genes using Hermitian adjacency (combining 24-hour fit quality and peak-time phase as complex values), inserts arrhythmic anchor genes via reliability-weighted nearest-neighbor projection, and ranks rhythmic genes using complex personalized PageRank on the magnetic Laplacian.
AI.result: Top-50 list shows 7.95x enrichment for KEGG circadian set (7/8 genes; p_BH=4e-6) and 4.54x for Reactome set (8/16 genes; p_BH=1.6e-4). Magnetic embedding recovers peak-time order of pituitary genes with accuracy 0.971 vs chance without magnetic charge. Phase-blind baseline recovers no enrichment.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively identifies clock-linked regulators of arrhythmic hormone genes through reliability weighting and phase-aware seeding, with magnetic phase providing temporal ordering capability that is essential for accurate recovery.

title: Trajectory Inference of Human Aging from Cross-Sectional DNA Methylation Data
summary: DNA methylation (DNAm) serves as one of the most robust molecular biomarkers of biological aging. While conventional epigenetic clocks accurately predict chronological age from high-dimensional CpG profiles, they treat aging as a static regression task, meaning they can only output a single score rather than simulating how an entire profile continuously changes over time. To reconstruct these continuous dynamics, we frame lifelong human epigenetic aging as a trajectory inference problem across discrete age snapshots derived from widely available cross-sectional data. We introduce a two-stage computational pipeline: first, an age-regularized Variational Autoencoder (VAE) maps high-dimensional CpG profiles onto a chronologically ordered latent manifold while preserving a generative decoder bridge back to the original methylation space. Second, we model the continuous movement across this latent space via Regularized Unbalanced Optimal Transport (RUOT) that unifies deterministic drift, random diffusion, and non-conservative mass changes. By resolving this RUOT formulation using the DeepRUOT framework, our model fluidly accommodates population-level density shifts like survivorship bias and cellular attrition without requiring rigid biological priors. Evaluated on a large-scale, 80-year pan-tissue dataset, our model demonstrates robust distribution interpolation and uncovers a prominent late-life surge in the learned growth field that mathematically captures the variance expansion driven by stochastic epigenetic drift. Finally, by decoding continuous latent paths back to individual CpG sites, we reconstruct and empirically verify distinct biological aging archetypes, offering a rigorous, generative paradigm for simulating human molecular aging.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a two-stage computational pipeline combining an age-regularized VAE with DeepRUOT (Regularized Unbalanced Optimal Transport) to reconstruct continuous epigenetic aging dynamics from cross-sectional DNA methylation data, revealing late-life stochastic drift surges and distinct aging archetypes.
AI.motivation: Conventional epigenetic clocks treat aging as a static regression task, outputting only a single score per sample. The authors aim to move beyond this by reconstructing continuous, dynamic trajectories of how entire DNAm profiles evolve over a lifetime.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) an age-regularized VAE projects high-dimensional CpG profiles onto a chronologically ordered latent manifold with a generative decoder; (2) Regularized Unbalanced Optimal Transport (via DeepRUOT) models continuous movement in this latent space, unifying deterministic drift, random diffusion, and non-conservative mass changes to handle survivorship bias and cellular attrition.
AI.result: Evaluated on an 80-year pan-tissue dataset, the model achieves robust distribution interpolation and reveals a prominent late-life surge in the learned growth field, mathematically capturing variance expansion from stochastic epigenetic drift. Distinct biological aging archetypes are reconstructed and empirically verified.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a rigorous, generative paradigm for simulating human molecular aging continuously, moving beyond static scoring to trajectory-level modeling of epigenetic dynamics.

title: Polynomial encoding of rooted trees with branch lengths
summary: Phylogenetic trees are rooted trees with branch lengths that record genetic divergence or elapsed time, and quantifying differences between them is central to a wide range of evolutionary and epidemiological analyses. Graph-polynomial encodings of rooted trees provide an accurate, interpretable, and computationally efficient way to compare tree shapes, but existing polynomial encodings must be paired with auxiliary structures to study rooted trees with branch lengths. We introduce a bivariate polynomial encoding that incorporates branch lengths directly into a recursive computation from the leaf vertices to the root vertex of a tree. We prove that, for rooted trees with branch lengths and no vertices of degree two, which include all standard phylogenetic trees, two trees have the same polynomial if and only if their underlying unlabeled trees are isomorphic and the branch lengths of corresponding edges are equal. We apply the polynomial encoding to three published HIV-1 phylogenies sampled in different epidemiological settings and show that it accurately separates the three datasets based on their tree topologies and branch lengths, outperforming previous polynomial-based approaches for analyzing rooted trees with branch lengths.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a bivariate polynomial encoding for rooted phylogenetic trees with branch lengths, proving it uniquely captures both tree topology and edge lengths (for trees without degree-2 vertices), and demonstrates its superior performance in separating HIV-1 phylogenies from different epidemiological settings.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-polynomial encodings for comparing tree shapes are accurate and efficient but require auxiliary structures to handle branch lengths. The authors aim to develop a unified encoding that directly incorporates branch lengths into the polynomial representation.
AI.method: A bivariate polynomial is recursively computed from leaves to root, integrating branch lengths directly. The encoding is proven to uniquely determine both the unlabeled tree topology and the branch lengths of corresponding edges, under the condition that the tree has no degree-2 vertices.
AI.result: Applied to three published HIV-1 phylogenies from different epidemiological settings, the encoding accurately separates the datasets based on topology and branch lengths, outperforming previous polynomial-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The bivariate polynomial provides a more powerful and interpretable tool for comparing rooted phylogenetic trees with branch lengths, with demonstrated utility in viral evolutionary analyses.

title: Selection Mechanisms, Stationary Distributions, and Reversibility in Multiallelic Moran Models
summary: The Moran process with selection and recurrent mutation is a classical model in population genetics, yet how the placement of selection within the update rule shapes the stationary distribution has received little attention. We study a finite, well-mixed haploid population of constant size $n$ with $m$ labeled alleles, parent-independent mutation, and allele-specific fitnesses. Within this common framework we compare three Moran update kernels that differ only in the stage at which selection acts: during reproduction, when the offspring copies one of two sampled parents (Scheme~I); through fitness-biased mate choice, followed by neutral copying (Scheme~II); and at death, so that fitter individuals are less likely to be replaced (Scheme~III). Although all three favor fitter alleles, they define different Markov chains. For two alleles, each scheme reduces to a birth-death chain and admits an exact stationary law, but the three laws differ. For $m\ge 3$, the placement of selection becomes decisive: Schemes~I and~II are generally nonreversible when fitnesses are unequal, so no detailed-balance product form exists, whereas Scheme~III remains reversible for every $m$ and has a closed stationary distribution -- a Dirichlet-multinomial core modified by an explicit fitness factor. We further show that all three mechanisms can act simultaneously in the two-allele case without losing exact solvability, and we derive weak-selection expansions that make explicit how small fitness differences tilt the neutral beta-binomial and Dirichlet-multinomial benchmarks. Together, these results clarify when neutral stationary structure survives the introduction of selection and when multiallelic Moran dynamics become genuinely nonreversible

AI.tldr: This paper compares three Moran process update schemes (selection during reproduction, mate choice, or death) and shows they yield different stationary distributions despite all favoring fitter alleles; Scheme III is reversible with closed-form solution, while Schemes I and II become nonreversible for 3+ alleles.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses a gap in the literature: although the Moran process with selection and mutation is a classical population genetics model, how the timing/placement of selection within the update rule shapes the stationary distribution has received little attention.
AI.method: The authors study a finite, well-mixed haploid population (constant size n) with m labeled alleles, parent-independent mutation, and allele-specific fitnesses. They compare three Moran update kernels differing only in selection placement: (I) during reproduction, (II) through fitness-biased mate choice, (III) at death. They derive exact stationary laws for the two-allele case and analyze reversibility for multiallelic cases.
AI.result: For two alleles, all three schemes reduce to birth-death chains with exact stationary laws, but the three laws differ. For m≥3, Schemes I and II are nonreversible with no detailed-balance product form, while Scheme III remains reversible for every m and has a closed Dirichlet-multinomial stationary distribution modified by a fitness factor. All three mechanisms can act simultaneously in the two-allele case with exact solvability preserved.
AI.conclusion: The results clarify when neutral stationary structure survives the introduction of selection and when multiallelic Moran dynamics become genuinely nonreversible, providing weak-selection expansions that show how small fitness differences tilt neutral benchmarks.

title: Enhanced sampling and cryo-EM data resolve magnesium binding to RNA
comment: Main text: 25 pages (5 figures); Supplementary Information: 24 pages (14 figures, 3 tables)
summary: Magnesium ions are essential for RNA structure but difficult to model due to slow binding kinetics and experimental limitations. We present an enhanced-sampling strategy that accelerates Mg$^{2+}$ inner-shell binding by orders of magnitude, enabling quantitative exploration of ion-binding motifs in a large ribozyme. The method combines a barrier-flattening bias with Hamiltonian replica exchange to efficiently sample multiple equivalent binding sites, and builds on an approach that achieved top performance in the CASP16 blind assessment of RNA solvation structure. Using cryo-electron microscopy maps for validation, we introduce a local analysis framework that infers the population of individual binding motifs from their agreement with experimental density, enabling site-by-site validation. We find that insufficient sampling of inner-shell binding leads to significantly poorer agreement with experiment, whereas force fields predicting different inner/outer binding equilibria remain largely indistinguishable at the current experimental resolution. These results highlight the dominant role of sampling in modelling divalent ion binding and provide a general strategy for integrating simulations with experimental data in complex biomolecular systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an enhanced-sampling method that accelerates Mg²⁺ inner-shell binding simulations by orders of magnitude, enabling quantitative study of ion-binding motifs in a large ribozyme. It shows sampling quality, not force field differences, is the dominant factor affecting agreement with experimental cryo-EM data.
AI.motivation: Mg²⁺ ions are essential for RNA structure but difficult to model computationally due to slow binding kinetics and experimental limitations, creating a need for better sampling methods to accurately study ion-binding motifs.
AI.method: Combines a barrier-flattening bias with Hamiltonian replica exchange to efficiently sample multiple equivalent binding sites, building on a top-performing CASP16 approach. Uses cryo-EM maps for validation with a local analysis framework that infers individual binding motif populations from experimental density agreement.
AI.result: Insufficient sampling of inner-shell binding leads to significantly poorer agreement with experiment. Force fields predicting different inner/outer binding equilibria remain largely indistinguishable at current experimental resolution.
AI.conclusion: Sampling quality is the dominant factor in modelling divalent ion binding; the study provides a general strategy for integrating simulations with experimental data in complex biomolecular systems.

title: Bayesian Learning of Distance Metrics Beyond RMSD for Biomolecule Alignment, Clustering, and Domain Identification
summary: Root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) is the standard metric of structural comparison in molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. In its conventional form, RMSD assigns equal weight to all atoms regardless of mobility. Hence, flexible loops and disordered regions can dominate a global RMSD, while the rigid functional core contributes negligibly to the overall metric. To address this issue, we introduce the Bayes-optimal RMSD (BRMSD), which optimizes per-atom weights jointly with structural averages by maximizing a Bayesian posterior. In a trade-off between low RMSD and weight uniformity, a position-fluctuation parameter $σ$ controls the transition from classical RMSD ($σ\to \infty$) to a progressive focus on a rigid core ($σ\to 0$). The BRMSD framework supports analysis modules for structural alignment, focused alignment onto a user-specified domain, trajectory smoothing, soft $K$-means conformational clustering, and rigid-domain identification. These modules are implemented in the open-source Python package BRMSD (https://github.com/bio-phys/BRMSD) and benchmarked on two MD systems, the endoplasmic reticulum translocon-associated protein SND3 and the phosphotransferase adenylate kinase.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Bayes-optimal RMSD (BRMSD), a method that assigns per-atom weights to improve structural comparison in MD simulations by focusing on rigid functional cores rather than flexible regions, implemented as an open-source Python package and benchmarked on two protein systems.
AI.motivation: Conventional RMSD gives equal weight to all atoms, causing flexible loops and disordered regions to dominate the metric while the rigid functional core contributes negligibly, which is problematic for analyzing MD simulations.
AI.method: BRMSD optimizes per-atom weights jointly with structural averages by maximizing a Bayesian posterior, with a position-fluctuation parameter σ controlling the trade-off between classical RMSD (σ→∞) and focus on rigid core (σ→0). Includes modules for alignment, clustering, and domain identification.
AI.result: Implemented as open-source Python package (BRMSD) and benchmarked on two MD systems: ER translocon-associated protein SND3 and adenylate kinase.
AI.conclusion: BRMSD provides a principled Bayesian approach to weighted RMSD that can focus on structurally rigid regions, addressing a key limitation of conventional RMSD in MD analysis.

title: Reliable mechanistic operator recovery with biologically-informed neural networks: principles for architecture and optimisation design
summary: Many biological processes are governed by complex dynamical mechanisms that remain incompletely understood despite increasing volumes of experimental data. Biologically-informed neural networks (BINNs) seek to address this challenge by embedding mechanistic differential equations into neural network training, enabling interpretable constitutive operators to be recovered directly from sparse and noisy observations. However, reliable operator recovery depends sensitively on network architecture, optimisation strategy, and data informativeness. Here, we present a systematic empirical study of how these factors influence mechanistic inference using BINNs applied to canonical one-dimensional advection-diffusion-reaction partial differential equation models. Across a suite of benchmark problems, we investigate how network expressivity, learning rate, loss weighting, and batch size influence optimisation behaviour and operator recovery. We show that successful mechanistic inference depends on balancing competing objectives rather than maximising any single aspect of the model or optimisation. Moderately expressive architectures outperform overly complex networks, intermediate learning rates improve optimisation stability, balanced data and PDE losses are essential for accurate operator recovery, and intermediate batch sizes provide the best compromise between computational efficiency and reproducibility. We further identify practical diagnostics for recognising common failure modes, including over-fitting, unstable optimisation, and poor mechanistic recovery when the ground truth is unavailable. Together, these findings provide evidence-based guidelines for deploying BINNs as credible tools for biological model discovery.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic empirical study of how network architecture, optimisation strategy, and data informativeness influence mechanistic inference using Biologically-informed neural networks (BINNs) applied to one-dimensional advection-diffusion-reaction PDE models, finding that balanced configurations (moderate architecture, intermediate learning rates, balanced loss weighting, intermediate batch sizes) yield the best operator recovery.
AI.motivation: Many biological processes are governed by complex dynamical mechanisms that remain incompletely understood despite increasing experimental data volumes. BINNs address this by embedding mechanistic differential equations into neural network training, but reliable operator recovery depends sensitively on various factors that lack systematic guidelines.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study using BINNs applied to canonical one-dimensional advection-diffusion-reaction PDE models, investigating network expressivity, learning rate, loss weighting, and batch size across benchmark problems.
AI.result: Moderately expressive architectures outperform overly complex networks; intermediate learning rates improve stability; balanced data and PDE losses are essential; intermediate batch sizes offer best efficiency-reproducibility trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Successful mechanistic inference depends on balancing competing objectives rather than maximizing any single aspect. The findings provide evidence-based guidelines for deploying BINNs as credible tools for biological model discovery, along with practical diagnostics for recognizing common failure modes.

title: A hierarchical memory architecture overcomes context limits in long-horizon multi-agent computational modeling
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Preprint submitted for publication
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet their stateless architecture fundamentally limits deployment in long-horizon research workflows requiring multi-session continuity and quantitative rigor. Here we present Ensemble QSP, a multi-agent framework featuring a three-layer hierarchical memory architecture that keeps injected context bounded and constant in project duration (mid-term project state: median 301 tokens, max 4,050, across 104 runs) by capping each state category and evicting completed work, enabling continuous autonomous operation without context degradation. The system orchestrates five specialist worker agents under domain-expert principal investigators, enforcing physical constraints through physics-based checklists and structured-domain knowledge. Comprehensive benchmarking demonstrates robust autonomous pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic model selection without human intervention, consistent result quality across both lower-cost and frontier LLMs, improved PK parameter recovery relative to single-agent baselines, and stable model selection across linguistically diverse prompts of the same task. Feature-level ablation across physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models spanning a broad complexity range shows that PI-agent oversight improves debugging efficiency while preserving final accuracy across conditions. The architecture is structurally domain-agnostic, adding a new scientific domain requires only a new PI agent configuration.

AI.tldr: Ensemble QSP introduces a multi-agent LLM framework with a three-layer hierarchical memory that caps context size (median 301 tokens), enabling continuous autonomous operation for long-horizon scientific workflows without context degradation, demonstrated on pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic model selection.
AI.motivation: LLMs have stateless architectures limiting deployment in long-horizon research workflows requiring multi-session continuity and quantitative rigor.
AI.method: Three-layer hierarchical memory architecture (capping each state category, evicting completed work to keep context bounded and constant); five specialist worker agents under domain-expert principal investigators; physics-based checklists and structured-domain knowledge.
AI.result: Robust autonomous PK-PD model selection without human intervention; consistent quality across lower-cost and frontier LLMs; improved PK parameter recovery vs. single-agent baselines; stable model selection across linguistically diverse prompts; PI-agent oversight improves debugging efficiency while preserving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The architecture is domain-agnostic; adding a new scientific domain requires only a new PI agent configuration.

title: Rethinking the Choice Behavior of Sugar Metabolism in Bacteria
summary: Ramkrishna, Kompala, and Tsao proposed the cybernetic model of microbial growth, in which cells allocate enzyme synthesis resources according to a matching rule that mimics rational decision-making. The matching rule was later shown to be optimal under general assumptions about the underlying return-on-investment structure, yet the specific objective the cell maximizes, and the constraints bounding that choice, were never written down as an explicit economic decision. Here we supply that missing decision, recasting cybernetic enzyme-synthesis control as a consumer choice problem from microeconomic theory: the cell allocates a limited proteome budget among competing catabolic enzymes as a linear program (LP), maximizing a linear growth utility subject to a linear proteome budget constraint. Because the utility is linear, the LP's solution is geometric: whenever the iso-utility line's slope differs from the budget constraint's, the optimum is a corner, and the entire proteome budget is allocated to the enzyme for the single most profitable substrate. Corner solutions correspond to diauxic growth, and sequential substrate consumption follows from the choice of corner rather than a distinct regulatory mechanism. Only when the two slopes coincide does the optimum spread across the entire budget line instead of concentrating at a single corner; this degenerate case underlies simultaneous substrate use. Using only parameters estimated independently from single-substrate experiments, the LP-derived cybernetic variables reproduced the diauxic and triauxic batch growth of Klebsiella oxytoca on glucose-xylose and glucose-xylose-lactose mixtures, achieving a fit comparable to the classical matching law. Thus, sequential substrate use is the generic outcome of growth-maximizing specialization under perfect substitutability, and co-utilization is the degenerate case of equal profitability.

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates the cybernetic model of microbial growth as a linear programming (LP) consumer choice problem, where cells maximize growth utility under a proteome budget constraint, showing that diauxic (sequential) growth emerges from corner solutions and simultaneous substrate use from degenerate equal-profitability cases.
AI.motivation: To provide an explicit economic decision framework underlying the cybernetic model's matching rule, which was known to be optimal but lacked a formal statement of the objective and constraints the cell maximizes.
AI.method: The authors cast enzyme-synthesis control as a linear program (LP) from microeconomic theory: cells allocate a limited proteome budget among catabolic enzymes, maximizing linear growth utility subject to a linear budget constraint. The LP solution is geometric—corner solutions vs. budget line solutions depending on slope alignment.
AI.result: Using independently estimated parameters from single-substrate experiments, the LP-derived cybernetic variables accurately reproduced diauxic and triauxic batch growth of Klebsiella oxytoca on glucose-xylose and glucose-xylose-lactose mixtures, with fit comparable to the classical matching law.
AI.conclusion: Sequential substrate use is the generic outcome of growth-maximizing specialization under perfect substitutability, while co-utilization is the degenerate case of equal profitability.

title: Reward Valuation in Vision Language Models: Causal Mechanisms Underlying Anhedonia
summary: Recent Vision-Language Models capture increasingly complex aspects of human cognition. Here we ask whether this alignment extends to reward valuation, which we assess in a mechanistic framework built on clinical tests that were developed to evaluate anhedonia and motivational deficits in major depressive disorder. In the brain, anhedonia is frequently linked to dysregulation in the Nucleus Accumbens (NAc) and the broader dopaminergic reward system. While neuroimaging has localized these deficits, establishing a causal link between NAc activity and specific behavioral symptoms remains a challenge. We use these ideas from neuroscience to functionally identify reward-anticipatory units in vision language models, and test their causal role via targeted perturbations. Perturbing NAc-selective units induces behavioral effects that mirror human anhedonia: the model shifts toward low-effort, low-reward options in effort-based decision-making tasks. Crucially, our results reflect a specific deficit in reward valuation and anticipation rather than a loss of task capability: the perturbed model maintains baseline performance when reward-based choice is removed. This induced vulnerability further aligns with clinical anhedonia and motivation scales, including DARS and MAP-SR. Taken together, these results reveal reward valuation circuits in AI models that parallel those in humans.

AI.tldr: VLMs have reward valuation circuits analogous to human brain reward systems; perturbing model counterparts of Nucleus Accumbens units induces anhedonia-like behavior mirroring human clinical symptoms.
AI.motivation: To test whether Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit alignment with human reward valuation mechanisms, using a mechanistic framework adapted from clinical neuroscience tests for anhedonia in depression.
AI.method: Functionally identified reward-anticipatory units in VLMs analogous to human NAc/dopaminergic reward system, then performed targeted perturbations on these units and evaluated behavioral changes in effort-based decision-making tasks.
AI.result: Perturbing NAc-selective units caused the model to shift toward low-effort, low-reward options, mirroring human anhedonia. The effect was specific to reward valuation—baseline task performance remained intact when reward-based choice was removed. Results aligned with clinical anhedonia scales (DARS, MAP-SR).
AI.conclusion: VLMs contain reward valuation circuits that functionally parallel human brain reward systems, and targeted perturbations can produce clinically-relevant anhedonia-like deficits.

title: STST-JEPA: Shallow-Target Spatio-Temporal Joint Embedding Prediction Architecture For EEG Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Brain age -- the age inferred from a physiological recording -- is an emerging biomarker whose deviation from chronological age tracks neurological and psychiatric burden, and EEG is an attractive substrate for it because it is cheap, portable, and temporally rich. Yet EEG brain-age models must contend with cross-site montage heterogeneity, small labelled cohorts, and dominant subject-level non-stationarity, and few EEG foundation models have been shown to deliver competitive age regression across the full pediatric-to-older-adult range in which such a biomarker would actually be deployed. We introduce STST-JEPA, a self-supervised transformer for resting-state and task EEG, pretrained on 47,703 sessions spanning ages 5-81 from the brain.space and Healthy Brain Network (HBN) corpora. The model combines a latent-prediction objective - predicting masked-token representations against an EMA-of-tokenizer target - with an auxiliary signal-reconstruction term, applied to 30-second multi-channel windows under spatiotemporal block masks. A lightweight attentive probe trained on frozen pretrained embeddings achieves a best held-out-validation mean absolute error of 3.06 years (r = 0.924) for age regression on 3,367 sessions, against a predict-the-mean baseline of approximately 10 years MAE. With light task-specific fine-tuning of the model's final layers, the same pretrained encoder achieves rank-1 placements - with the model's native 30-second windows - on the public NeuralBench x brain.space EEG leaderboard for sex classification (balanced accuracy 0.911), age prediction (r = 0.749), and psychopathology composite regression (r = 0.215). We further show that the model's age-prediction residual is negatively correlated with cognitive efficiency over several tasks we examined.

AI.tldr: STST-JEPA is a self-supervised EEG transformer pretrained on 47,703 sessions (ages 5-81) that achieves state-of-the-art age regression (MAE 3.06 years, r=0.924) and rank-1 results on sex classification, age prediction, and psychopathology regression on the NeuralBench leaderboard, using masked latent prediction and signal reconstruction objectives.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust EEG-based brain-age biomarker that works across the full pediatric-to-older-adult age range despite challenges like cross-site montage heterogeneity, small labeled datasets, and subject-level non-stationarity in EEG recordings.
AI.method: A self-supervised transformer (STST-JEPA) combining a latent-prediction objective (predicting masked-token representations against an EMA-of-tokenizer target) with an auxiliary signal-reconstruction term, applied to 30-second multi-channel windows under spatiotemporal block masks, pretrained on a large corpus of 47,703 sessions.
AI.result: Best held-out MAE of 3.06 years (r=0.924) for age regression on 3,367 sessions; rank-1 on NeuralBench leaderboard for sex classification (balanced accuracy 0.911), age prediction (r=0.749), and psychopathology composite regression (r=0.215); age-prediction residual negatively correlates with cognitive efficiency.
AI.conclusion: STST-JEPA demonstrates that self-supervised pretraining on large-scale EEG data can produce a versatile foundation model for multiple clinically relevant biomarkers, outperforming prior approaches across age regression, sex classification, and psychopathology assessment tasks.

title: Dimension-dependent continuum limits in tissue mechanics
summary: Continuum descriptions of epithelial tissue mechanics can replace expensive individual-based simulations with tractable macroscopic models, yet the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport remains poorly understood. We show that dimensionality controls this link: long-time mechanical relaxation rates reveal generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport phenomena, $D(ρ)\proptoρ^γ$. Exponents in nonlinear diffusivities are fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality, providing a novel physical mechanism for emergent macroscopic transport phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that dimensionality controls the link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport, revealing that mechanical relaxation rates follow generalized porous-media-type nonlinear diffusion, with exponents fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport in epithelial tissue mechanics, replacing expensive individual-based simulations with tractable macroscopic models.
AI.method: The study analyzes how dimensionality controls the link between microscopic mechanics and macroscopic transport, deriving nonlinear diffusivities D(ρ) ∝ ρ^γ from long-time mechanical relaxation rates.
AI.result: Long-time mechanical relaxation rates reveal generalized porous-media-type nonlinear transport phenomena where D(ρ) ∝ ρ^γ, with exponents fixed by microscopic mechanics and dimensionality.
AI.conclusion: Dimensionality provides a novel physical mechanism for emergent macroscopic transport phenomena in epithelial tissues, establishing a direct link between cell-scale forces and tissue-scale transport.

title: Resource-Efficient Hybrid Quantum Neighborhood Selection for Large-Scale Molecular Diversity Optimization
summary: Large-scale combinatorial optimization remains demanding for classical heuristics, particularly when dense Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulations induce large memory footprints, high CPU utilization, and long execution times. While near-term quantum processors cannot yet deliver unconditional quantum advantage, hybrid architectures can provide practical value by reducing the resource burden. This paper presents a resource-efficiency study of Hybrid Quantum Neighborhood Selection (HQNS), a framework that decomposes large dense QUBO instances into bounded-width quantum subproblems via stochastic frontier selection. We evaluate HQNS on the Maximum Diversity Subset Selection Problem (MDSSP), focusing on the trade-off between solution quality retention and resource consumption. Benchmarks up to N=1000 candidates show that HQNS preserves 99.9908% of the mean diversity score of an 11-restart parallel Simulated Annealing baseline, while reducing wall-clock time by 94.91%, peak CPU utilization by 64.68%, and peak memory usage by 88.61%. The QPU execution time remains bounded within a 6-7 second envelope across scales, indicating that the quantum component is decoupled from the global QUBO dimension when the frontier size is fixed. These results suggest that HQNS provides a resource-aware pathway for deploying hybrid quantum optimization in practical large-scale settings, serving as an efficient architecture for incorporating near-term quantum processors into classical optimization pipelines.

AI.tldr: HQNS decomposes large dense QUBO problems into smaller quantum subproblems, preserving 99.99% solution quality while drastically reducing computational resources (95% time, 65% CPU, 89% memory).
AI.motivation: Large-scale combinatorial optimization with dense QUBO formulations suffers from high memory, CPU, and time costs. Classical heuristics struggle, and pure quantum approaches aren't yet viable, motivating hybrid resource-efficient solutions.
AI.method: HQNS (Hybrid Quantum Neighborhood Selection) uses stochastic frontier selection to decompose large dense QUBO instances into bounded-width quantum subproblems, evaluated on the MDSSP benchmark.
AI.result: On N=1000 benchmarks: 99.9908% mean diversity score retention, 94.91% wall-clock reduction, 64.68% CPU reduction, 88.61% memory reduction. QPU time stays within 6-7 seconds regardless of problem size.
AI.conclusion: HQNS offers a resource-aware pathway for deploying hybrid quantum optimization in practical large-scale settings, effectively decoupling quantum execution from global problem dimension.

title: Single-Entity Spiking Neuron Models: Survey
summary: In this work, we reviewed different approaches in mathematical modeling of biologically plausible neural systems. Models are characterized and classified based on their common features and special use cases. In addition to spiking models, different types of discrete and continuous analogs are considered to accurately simulate biological processes, including membrane potential dynamics. The models under investigation include neurons and various components encountered in neural systems and affected the dynamics. The selection of specific approaches was driven by their prevalence and innovative perspectives in order to enhance the relevance of the presented information.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews and classifies mathematical models of biologically plausible neural systems, covering spiking models, discrete/continuous analogs, and components affecting neural dynamics, selected based on prevalence and innovation.
AI.motivation: To provide a comprehensive overview and classification of mathematical modeling approaches for biologically realistic neural systems, helping researchers understand the landscape of available models.
AI.method: The authors reviewed and characterized various mathematical models of neural systems, classifying them based on common features and use cases, including spiking models and discrete/continuous analogs that simulate biological processes like membrane potential dynamics.
AI.result: A classification framework for neural models was developed, covering different types of models (spiking, discrete, continuous) and their applications to various neural system components.
AI.conclusion: The selection of models was driven by prevalence and innovative perspectives to enhance the relevance of the presented information for modeling biologically plausible neural systems.

title: Collaborate to decorrelate in path space: Hamiltonian replica exchange transition interface sampling (HRETIS)
summary: We present Hamiltonian Replica Exchange Transition Interface Sampling (HRETIS), a path sampling framework designed to efficiently sample rare events in systems with complex potential energy landscapes. HRETIS introduces a helper potential within a Hamiltonian replica exchange scheme, which enhances exploration of path space when the underlying potential is not well suited for conventional path sampling approaches. This is particularly advantageous for systems exhibiting multiple pathways separated by orthogonal barriers such as in drug (un)binding, where standard algorithms often show slow convergence since they become trapped within specific pathways. By exchanging Hamiltonians between the path ensembles, HRETIS overcomes these limitations and increases the decorrelation between subsequent paths in the Monte Carlo chain. We demonstrate that HRETIS provides robust and accurate kinetics in several systems, including coarse-grained simulations of amino acid permeation through a dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) membrane. Moreover, HRETIS is found to improve sampling efficiency and convergence, illustrating its potential as a powerful tool for rare event sampling in complex molecular systems.

AI.tldr: HRETIS is a path sampling framework that uses Hamiltonian replica exchange with a helper potential to efficiently sample rare events (like drug binding/unbinding) in complex molecular systems, demonstrating improved convergence and accurate kinetics in simulations of amino acid permeation through DPPC membranes.
AI.motivation: Standard path sampling algorithms converge slowly for complex systems with multiple pathways separated by orthogonal barriers (e.g., drug (un)binding) because they become trapped within specific pathways.
AI.method: HRETIS introduces a helper potential within a Hamiltonian replica exchange scheme to enhance path space exploration. Hamiltonians are exchanged between path ensembles to improve decorrelation between subsequent paths in the Monte Carlo chain.
AI.result: HRETIS provides robust and accurate kinetics in coarse-grained simulations of amino acid permeation through DPPC membranes, and improves sampling efficiency and convergence.
AI.conclusion: HRETIS is a powerful tool for rare event sampling in complex molecular systems, overcoming the limitations of conventional path sampling approaches.

title: Equivalence testing in pesticide risk assessment -- Evaluation and practical guidance for design, analysis and interpretation
summary: Harmful pesticide effects exceeding specific protection goals (SPG) may go undetected in underpowered experimental designs. Regulatory honeybee field studies have consistently failed to reach the statistical power required under European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) guidance, which may have caused approval of high-risk substances. Therefore, EFSA advised a shift from testing the null hypothesis of 'no effect' to equivalence testing. Under this approach, a pesticide is classified as 'low risk' if the null hypothesis that its effect exceeds the SPG can be rejected. For honeybees, the recommended SPG is a colony size reduction below 10%. Critics have argued that this framework requires excessive site replication to demonstrate pesticide safety and proposed an alternative equivalence test defining treatment effects relative to the lower bound of the 90%-control-group confidence interval. Using simulations mimicking a regulatory honeybee field study, we show that although the two equivalence tests share the same trade-off between false 'low-risk' and false 'high-risk' classifications, only EFSA's original recommendation reliably identifies pesticides with effects > SPG at alpha = 0.2. Our results show that increasing site replication beyond the current practice is unavoidable for a reliable regulatory assessment. However, for pesticides with effect sizes of 5% or less, site requirements remain lower than those implied by the power requirement of the former EFSA guidance. Moreover, covariate adjustment through a model term or balanced colony allocation using anticlustering randomisation can reduce site requirements without losing power and thus save costs. Finally, we provide guidance and R functions for anticlustering randomisation and equivalence testing for pesticide risk assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that EFSA's proposed equivalence testing framework for honeybee pesticide risk assessment requires more sites than current practice but fewer than old power-based guidance. The original EFSA method reliably detects effects >10% SPG at alpha=0.2, while alternative tests fail. Covariate adjustment and anticlustering randomisation can reduce site requirements and costs.
AI.motivation: Regulatory honeybee field studies under current null hypothesis testing are underpowered, potentially allowing approval of harmful pesticides. EFSA advised shifting to equivalence testing but critics argued it requires excessive replication. The paper aims to evaluate these competing equivalence testing frameworks.
AI.method: Simulations mimicking a regulatory honeybee field study were used to compare EFSA's original equivalence test (null: effect exceeds SPG of 10% colony size reduction) against a proposed alternative (treatment effect relative to lower bound of 90% control CI). Covariate adjustment and anticlustering randomisation were evaluated for efficiency gains.
AI.result: Both tests share the same false classification trade-offs, but only EFSA's original reliably detects effects >10% SPG at alpha=0.2. Increased site replication is unavoidable, but for effects ≤5%, requirements are lower than under old EFSA guidance. Covariate adjustment and anticlustering randomisation reduce site needs without losing power.
AI.conclusion: Equivalence testing with EFSA's original method is feasible with increased site replication, and efficiency gains from anticlustering and covariate adjustment can reduce costs. The authors provide R functions and guidance for implementation.

title: Directional bias of a single polarized cell under confinement
summary: Chiral patterns have been observed in various processes from swirling bacterial colonies to tissue morphogenesis and cytoskeletal organization, yet the physical mechanisms underlying chiral cell motion remain poorly understood. Motivated by experiments demonstrating directional bias in the circular motion of confined cells, we use the tools of dynamical systems analysis with computer simulations to identify minimal intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms capable of generating persistent biased migration. The dynamical systems framework reveals a common organizing principle: directional bias emerges through changes in the stability and/or basins of attraction of the clockwise and counter-clockwise motility states. We find four distinct routes to such bias. First, intrinsic torque in a polarized cytoskeleton can be spatially integrated to produce biased circular motion. Second, anisotropic cell-substrate friction can generate directional preference when reduced friction along the polarity axis is coupled to a directional offset. Third, a chiral wall-alignment response can also produce a persistent directional preference. Finally, substrate patterns that break mirror symmetry, such as dextral or sinistral ridges and troughs, can likewise bias rotational direction. Together, these mechanisms yield distinct, testable predictions and suggest a unifying lens for experimental interrogation of cellular chirality and the design of synthetic systems with programmable chiral motion.

AI.tldr: This paper uses dynamical systems analysis and simulations to identify four distinct mechanisms by which chiral (directional) bias emerges in confined cell migration, organized around a common principle of altered stability/basins of attraction of clockwise vs. counter-clockwise motion states.
AI.motivation: Chiral patterns are observed in various biological processes (bacterial colonies, tissue morphogenesis, cytoskeletal organization), but the physical mechanisms underlying chiral cell motion remain poorly understood. The authors aim to identify minimal mechanisms that can generate persistent biased migration.
AI.method: The authors employ dynamical systems analysis combined with computer simulations to identify minimal intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms for biased migration. They use a framework examining stability and basins of attraction of clockwise and counter-clockwise motility states.
AI.result: Four distinct routes to directional bias are identified: (1) intrinsic torque from polarized cytoskeleton, (2) anisotropic cell-substrate friction with directional offset, (3) chiral wall-alignment response, and (4) mirror-symmetry-breaking substrate patterns (dextral/sinistral ridges).
AI.conclusion: The mechanisms yield distinct, testable predictions and provide a unifying framework for experimental study of cellular chirality and design of synthetic systems with programmable chiral motion.
